questions.question: Explain the difference between stack-based and heap-based buffer overflow attacks.
keyPoints: Stack-based overflow targets return addresses in stack frames
Heap-based overflow targets metadata in dynamically allocated memory
Stack attacks are easier to exploit due to predictable layout
Heap attacks require corrupting memory management structures

questions.question: What are stack canaries and how do they provide protection against buffer overflows?
keyPoints: Stack canaries are known values placed between buffer and return address
Placed at compile time using -fstack-protector flag
Check canary value before function returns
If modified, program terminates immediately
Does not prevent overflow but detects it before control is hijacked

questions.question: Describe three countermeasures against buffer overflow attacks with their purposes.
keyPoints: Stack Canaries - detect overflow by checking guard value
ASLR - randomize memory addresses to make exploits unpredictable
DEP/NX - mark data pages as non-executable
Safe Libraries - use bounds-checked functions
Code Hardening - compile with security flags

questions.question: What is integer overflow? How can it be exploited?
keyPoints: Integer overflow occurs when arithmetic result exceeds maximum representable value
Result wraps around to small or negative value
Can cause buffer overflows when used for memory allocation
Attacker manipulates size values to allocate smaller buffers
Write sanitizers and proper bounds checking prevent exploitation

questions.question: Explain the working of the Morris Worm and its impact on computer security.
keyPoints: Released in 1988 by Robert Morris
Exploited buffer overflow vulnerability in finger daemon
Affected approximately 6,000 computers (10% of internet at that time)
First major internet-wide security incident
Led to formation of CERTs and increased focus on secure coding

questions.question: What is Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)? Why is it effective against DEP?
keyPoints: ROP chains existing code fragments (gadgets) ending with RET instructions
Does not inject new code - uses legitimate code already in memory
Bypasses DEP by executing existing code rather than injected shellcode
Can bypass ASLR if sufficient gadgets found in non-randomized libraries
Modern defenses combine ROP mitigations with other techniques

questions.question: Differentiate between format string vulnerability and buffer overflow with an example.
keyPoints: Format string occurs when user input is used as format specifier
printf(user_input) allows %n to write memory
Buffer overflow writes beyond array boundaries
Example of format string: printf(input) where input contains %x %x %n
Example of buffer overflow: strcpy(buf, large_string) without size check

questions.question: Describe the complete process of a stack-based buffer overflow attack. Discuss all the countermeasures that can prevent or mitigate such attacks.
keyPoints: Attack process: Overflow local buffer, overwrite return address, redirect execution to shellcode
Vulnerability caused by unsafe functions like gets(), strcpy() without bounds checking
Countermeasures: Stack canaries (detect overflow)
DEP/NX bit (prevent code execution on stack)
ASLR (randomize addresses)
Safe libraries (bounds-checked functions)
Non-executable stack
Input validation
Compiler warnings and hardening flags
Defense in depth approach

questions.question: Explain the concept of Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) and its effectiveness against buffer overflow attacks. Discuss its limitations.
keyPoints: ASLR randomizes addresses of stack, heap, libraries, and executable
Makes exploit development harder as attacker cannot predict jump targets
Effectiveness depends on entropy bits available
Limitations: Information leaks can reveal addresses
Does not protect against overwrite of adjacent variables
Can be bypassed with brute force or memory leaks
Return-to-libc and ROP techniques work around ASLR
Works best when combined with DEP and stack canaries

questions.question: Write short notes on: (a) Heap-based buffer overflow (b) Integer overflow (c) Shellcode (d) Format string vulnerability
keyPoints: (a) Heap overflow: Corrupts heap metadata, overwrites function pointers, use-after-free attacks
(b) Integer overflow: Wraparound of arithmetic results, leads to buffer overflows via incorrect size calculations
(c) Shellcode: Machine code to spawn shell, position-independent, typically disables NX for stack execution
(d) Format string: User input as format string allows %n write, %s read, exploiting printf family functions

questions.question: Discuss the secure coding practices that developers should follow to prevent buffer overflow vulnerabilities in C/C++ programs.
keyPoints: Use safe library functions: strcpy_s, strncpy_s instead of strcpy, strcpy
Always specify buffer sizes when copying strings
Validate all user input before processing
Enable compiler warnings (-Wall, -Wextra)
Use compile-time hardening: -fstack-protector, -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2
Prefer higher-level languages with bounds checking
Use static analysis tools to detect vulnerabilities
Avoid dangerous functions: gets(), sprintf(), strcat()
Perform regular security audits and code reviews
